narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your high family income background and a solid LSAT score of 38, which stands out above the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 41.6% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades that year placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers, far below the average of about the 6th decile. This stood out strongly because later-year performance is a key signal of readiness for the intense bar exam. On a positive note, your LSAT score pushed firmly against failure, showing your strong analytical skills compared to most applicants. Your high family income also helped, suggesting resources that often support success, unlike the more modest averages in the dataset. Your Year 1 decile of 1 had a smaller but still negative pull toward failure, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.9—slightly below the typical 3.2—nudged things slightly in a better direction, and your full-time student status aligned with the vast majority who pass. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by excelling in test scores and background but lagging in school deciles, which outweighed the positives here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome early hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring for consistent performance, and reapply with updated transcripts showing improvement. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing.
